results.possibleIdentity: Pedro Lujan (born 1943) is a Chicano sculptor, video artist, and multimedia creator born in El Paso, Texas. After earning a BA from the University of Texas at El Paso in 1965, he moved to New York City in the mid-1960s, where he became active in the experimental art scene of the late 1960s and 1970s. Lujan is recognized for his wood sculptures and his early engagement with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.); he and E. C. Hyde were featured in an E.A.T. exhibition that ran from November 1968 to February 1969. He also collaborated with Davidson Gigliotti to design and build an inflatable media environment for the video collective Videofreex. His early experimental work included architectural installations, such as a large ropebridge, and multimedia projects like the video 'Viaje a Caballo' (1983), which documented a horseback journey along the Rio Grande. Lujan received an MA from Goddard College in 1973 and was a recipient of the Guggenheim Fellowship in sculpture (1982), as well as grants from the National Endowment for the Arts (1974, 1977). His work has been exhibited at institutions such as El Museo del Barrio, the Fondo del Sol Visual Arts Center, and the Just Above Midtown (JAM) gallery. He also participated in the Roswell Artist in Residence Program in 1981–82.

results.possibleIdentity: Bernar Venet (born Bernard Venet on April 20, 1941, in Château-Arnoux-Saint-Auban, France) is a prominent French conceptual artist and sculptor who became influential in the late 1960s and early 1970s. After moving to New York in 1966, he became a pioneer of Conceptual art, known for incorporating mathematics, scientific language, and logical diagrams into his work. He was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, participating in their landmark exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum in 1968. His early career featured unconventional materials such as tar, coal, and cardboard; notably his 1963 work 'Tas de charbon' (Pile of Coal) is considered the first sculpture without a specific shape. From 1971 to 1976, Venet famously ceased all artistic production for a period of 'theoretical reflection,' during which he taught Art Theory at the Sorbonne in Paris. He resumed his practice in 1976 and later gained international fame for his monumental steel sculptures of arcs, angles, and indeterminate lines. His work is held in major global collections including MoMA, the Guggenheim, and the Centre Pompidou, and in 2011 he became the fourth contemporary artist to have a solo exhibition at the Palace of Versailles.

results.possibleIdentity: Sheldon Levine is an engineer and technical collaborator who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, specifically noted for his involvement with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. He served as a key technical partner in the E.A.T. project "Utopia: Question and Answer" (also known as the Utopia Q&A project) in December 1971. This project was an ambitious cross-cultural telecommunications experiment that linked participants in New York, Tokyo, Stockholm, and Ahmedabad through telex communication, involving prominent artists such as Andy Warhol and Keith Sonnier. Levine's professional career was centered at AeroNav Laboratories (also known as Aero Nav Laboratories) in College Point, New York, where he served in leadership roles including Vice President for Marketing and Business Development. His expertise included environmental simulation testing and engineering failure analysis, topics on which he frequently lectured for organizations such as the IEEE and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) through the 2000s and 2010s.

results.possibleIdentity: Howard Gutstadt (also known as Howie Gutstadt) is a video artist, media activist, and technologist who was a prominent figure in the New York City video collective scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. He co-founded the People's Video Theater (PVT) in the early 1970s and later co-founded Survival Arts Media (SAM) in 1973, which evolved from PVT. His work primarily focused on video activism, community-based documentary, and early electronic video art. Gutstadt was a pioneer in using the Sony Portapak for social change and was involved in the early development of public access television in New York, organizing the Public Access Celebration. His archival footage from 1971, known as the "Camp Jened tapes," became the foundational material for the 2020 Netflix documentary 'Crip Camp', which chronicles the origins of the disability rights movement. Beyond activism, Gutstadt explored immersive multimedia performance and electronic image processing, working with early synthesizers like the Rutt-Etra and the Electronic Associates of Berkeley (EAB). In 1978, he relocated to Berkeley, California, where he established Digital Image Productions and collaborated with companies such as Sony and Autodesk to develop digital software tools for artists. He has also been active in the SIGGRAPH community, serving on the organizing committee for the 1985 Art Show.

results.possibleIdentity: Yehuda Ben Yehuda (also spelled Ben-Yehuda or Jehuda Ben-Jehuda) was an Israeli-born painter and sculptor who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. Born in Baghdad, Iraq, in 1932 or 1933, he moved to Israel as a child and later became a prominent figure in the New York City art scene, particularly in SoHo. His work in the mid-1960s included avant-garde sculptures made of latex casts of live people, which sometimes incorporated electric appliances to create a trembling effect, reflecting the era's interest in the intersection of art and technology. This experimentation is reflected in archival records that associate him with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement and other contemporary projects of that period. In the early 1970s, he was a key figure in the conversion of SoHo industrial lofts into artist-owned cooperatives. By 1972, he moved to Woodstock, New York, where he spent several years hand-building an 1,800-square-foot, four-story wooden house on the Little Beaverkill stream in Bearsville. His artistic career included exhibitions at the Tel Aviv Museum of Art (1960), the Haifa Museum of Art (1960), and the Chemerinsky Art Gallery (1966). He is the father of writer Noa Jones.
